experiment: LongMemEval-s retrieval: embedding-model A/B on the SAME first-50 questions (all single-session-user = the hardest type)
metric: session-level retrieval recall@k / hit@k / MRR of gold answer_session_ids; judge-free, no external API; fresh hermetic per-question store (never touches ~/.engram)

full_500_saved_unlabeled.note: benchmark/results/lme_s_k5_full.json — all 6 question types; model NOT recorded (the gap that motivated the runner model-tag fix). single-session-user is the hardest subset, which is why the 50-slice scores lower.

hypothesis_B4: e5 (multilingual) will NOT beat the English-tuned MiniLM on ENGLISH retrieval; the +52% MRR e5 win measured earlier was ITALIAN-specific.
verdict: CONFIRMED (direction). On English the two are TIED within noise: recall 0.800 vs 0.820 (= 1 question / 50), MRR 0.669 vs 0.666. The e5 flip bought a large Italian recall gain (MRR 0.466->0.710, +52%) at ZERO English regression — the right call for an IT-heavy corpus. But 'e5 is universally better' is FALSE.
honesty_caveats: RETRIEVAL sub-metric (recall@k of gold sessions), NOT end-to-end QA accuracy (which needs an LLM judge) -> NOT directly comparable to published Mem0/Zep QA-accuracy leaderboards.
50-question slice, all single-session-user (hardest type). Full-500 e5 re-run pending for the definitive current-model headline.
No head-to-head vs Mem0/Zep on an identical protocol yet -> cannot claim 'we beat X'.
session-granularity mapping; long sessions truncated by the tokenizer (Engram is tuned for short facts).
